clean_story: Ghostly particle paths.  Cosmic whispers in ice. "Neutrino trails" refers to the faint, fleeting marks left by neutrinos—particles so light and fast they pass through planets like sunlight through glass. If neutrinos can rightly be perceived as cosmic phantoms, their trails are the footprints they leave in the snow of a detector’s heart. Trillions zip through your body every second—yet almost never touch anything. But deep in Antarctica, the IceCube detector waits. A cubic kilometer of ultra-pure ice, studded with basketball-sized sensors. When a neutrino rarely bumps an atom, it sparks a flash of blue light—a trail like a shooting star frozen in time. One 2017 trail pointed back to a blazar, a galaxy 4 billion light-years away spewing neutrinos like a firehose. Another matched a dying star’s collapse in 1987—neutrinos arrived hours before the light did. These trails are history’s quietest messengers. No charge. Nearly no mass. Yet they carry secrets from the universe’s violent corners—unfiltered, undistorted. Three related subjects are cosmic rays, dark matter, and quantum ghosts.
